features.summary: Home Depot reported Q2 2021 sales of $41.1 billion, up 8.1% year-over-year, marking the first time quarterly sales exceeded $40 billion. Comparable sales increased 4.5% with U.S. comps up 3.4%. Diluted EPS grew to $4.53 from $4.20 in the prior year. The company saw shifting consumer patterns as the economy reopened, with weekday sales strengthening relative to weekends as consumers returned to travel and recreational activities. Pro customer sales outpaced DIY for the second consecutive quarter, with professionals reporting strong backlogs.
features.management_outlook: Management remains optimistic about long-term home improvement demand, citing supportive housing fundamentals and home price appreciation of over 20% in the past two years. They expect several years of healthy demand ahead, driven by strong homeowner balance sheets and mortgage finance conditions. The company continues building out its One Home Depot Supply Chain to deliver same/next-day service to 90% of the U.S. population by 2022. However, management acknowledges significant uncertainty around COVID-19 variants and their potential impact on consumer spending patterns.
features.risks: The company faces ongoing supply chain disruptions including raw material shortages, production constraints, and transportation pressures across all modes. Lumber price volatility created unprecedented gross margin pressure, with framing lumber prices swinging from $1,500 to $500 per thousand board feet during the quarter. Rising transportation costs and supply chain investments continue pressuring margins. There's uncertainty about sustaining growth rates as consumers return to pre-pandemic activities and whether DIY demand will weaken as people resume normal routines.
features.key_drivers: Pro customer strength is a major growth driver, with professionals reporting record backlogs and the National Association of Homebuilders remodeling index hitting all-time highs. Big-ticket transactions over $1,000 increased 24% year-over-year, indicating customers are taking on larger projects. The company's interconnected retail strategy continues gaining traction, with over 55% of online orders fulfilled through stores. Inventory levels improved with $18.9 billion in stock, up $5.4 billion from last year, helping capture market share despite industry-wide supply constraints.
